SUNNY_OUTDOOR.rationale: Low ISO preserves skin detail. f/2.8 creates a soft background separation. Fast shutter eliminates motion blur in bright conditions.
SUNNY_OUTDOOR.proTip: Position subject so the sun is slightly behind them and use a reflector or fill flash to balance the exposure.
SUNNY_OUTDOOR.commonMistake: Shooting with the sun directly behind you creates harsh, flat light and squinting subjects.

CLOUDY_OUTDOOR.rationale: Overcast skies act as a giant softbox—ideal diffused light for portraits. Bump ISO to compensate for reduced light.
CLOUDY_OUTDOOR.proTip: Cloudy days give beautiful, even lighting with soft shadows. Great for skin tones.
CLOUDY_OUTDOOR.commonMistake: Underexposing subjects because the sky reads as bright in evaluative metering—apply +0.7 EV compensation.

INDOOR_NATURAL.rationale: Large aperture maximizes light at f/2.0. Minimum shutter for handheld sharpness. IBIS adds extra cushion.
INDOOR_NATURAL.proTip: Position subject near a window for directional natural light. Turn off indoor lights to avoid mixed color temperatures.
INDOOR_NATURAL.commonMistake: Mixing window light with tungsten room lights creates unflattering color casts.

STUDIO_STANDARD.rationale: f/8 gives sharp focus across the face in a group. Flash sync at 1/160. Base ISO minimizes noise.
STUDIO_STANDARD.proTip: Use a light meter to nail your flash power. Set camera to manual and match the ambient to be dark, flash to expose properly.
STUDIO_STANDARD.commonMistake: Exceeding your camera's flash sync speed causes a black bar across the bottom of the frame.

NIGHT_AMBIENT.rationale: Maximum aperture and elevated ISO capture ambient city light without flash, preserving the mood.
NIGHT_AMBIENT.proTip: Find a pool of warm street light to illuminate your subject against the dark background.
NIGHT_AMBIENT.commonMistake: Camera shake at 1/60 is a real risk—brace yourself or use IBIS to compensate.

FAST_MOVEMENT.rationale: 1/500 freezes movement of walking, dancing, or active subjects. Continuous AF tracks the subject.
FAST_MOVEMENT.proTip: Use burst mode and keep the shutter half-pressed to maintain AF lock on moving subjects.
FAST_MOVEMENT.commonMistake: Leaving AF set to single-shot loses focus when the subject moves between shots.

BACKLIT_GOLDEN_HOUR.rationale: Expose for the subject's face using spot metering. Golden backlight creates beautiful rim lighting and hair glow.
BACKLIT_GOLDEN_HOUR.proTip: Use a reflector or small fill flash at -2 stops to open up shadows on the face while retaining the backlit effect.
BACKLIT_GOLDEN_HOUR.commonMistake: Letting the camera expose for the bright background silhouettes the subject.

GROUP_PORTRAIT.rationale: f/5.6 ensures everyone in the group stays sharp. Use burst mode to catch a shot with all eyes open.
GROUP_PORTRAIT.proTip: Ask everyone to look at you and shoot 5–10 frames in burst mode. Someone will always blink.
GROUP_PORTRAIT.commonMistake: Using too wide an aperture (f/1.8) means only the front row is in focus.

ENVIRONMENTAL.rationale: f/4 keeps the subject sharp while blurring the background enough to establish place without losing context.
ENVIRONMENTAL.proTip: Frame the subject small in the environment to tell a story about who they are and where they work.
ENVIRONMENTAL.commonMistake: Using f/1.4 erases all the environmental context that makes this style meaningful.

SILHOUETTE_SUNSET.rationale: Expose for the bright sky. Subject becomes a graphic black silhouette against the colorful sunset.
SILHOUETTE_SUNSET.proTip: Look for subjects with distinctive, recognizable outlines—a person jumping, a tree, or a cityscape profile.
SILHOUETTE_SUNSET.commonMistake: Metering for the subject blows out the sky and loses the dramatic effect.

LOW_KEY_PORTRAIT.rationale: Dark, moody image with selective lighting. Expose for the lit side of the face only.
LOW_KEY_PORTRAIT.proTip: Use a single directional light source and black cards to flag light away from the shadows.
LOW_KEY_PORTRAIT.commonMistake: Accidentally letting ambient light fill in shadows, destroying the low-key effect.

HIGH_KEY_PORTRAIT.rationale: Bright, light-filled image with minimal shadows. Overexpose slightly to achieve the airy aesthetic.
HIGH_KEY_PORTRAIT.proTip: Use a white or grey seamless backdrop and multiple light sources to eliminate shadows.
HIGH_KEY_PORTRAIT.commonMistake: Overexposing so much that you blow out highlights and lose facial detail.

CREATIVE_SHALLOW_DOF.rationale: Extreme shallow depth of field at f/1.4. Fast shutter required in daylight to avoid overexposure.
CREATIVE_SHALLOW_DOF.proTip: Eye detection AF is essential at f/1.4. Even 1cm of missed focus renders eyes unacceptably soft.
CREATIVE_SHALLOW_DOF.commonMistake: Not using eye-detection AF leads to ears in focus and eyes soft at extreme apertures.

FORMAL_HEADSHOTS.rationale: f/4 sharpens the entire face. Consistent exposure for a professional series.
FORMAL_HEADSHOTS.proTip: Use a grey card to set a custom white balance for consistent color across all frames in a headshot session.
FORMAL_HEADSHOTS.commonMistake: Wild variation in exposure and white balance between shots makes post-processing a nightmare.

CINEMATIC_STYLED.rationale: 180-degree rule shutter (ISO 400 at f/2.8). Widescreen crop in post achieves the cinematic look.
CINEMATIC_STYLED.proTip: Apply a film-inspired LUT in post. Slightly underexpose 1/3 stop for that filmic, moody quality.
CINEMATIC_STYLED.commonMistake: Over-sharpening in post fights the cinematic aesthetic—pull clarity and sharpness down slightly.

BLACK_AND_WHITE.rationale: Shoot RAW for maximum conversion flexibility. f/4 gives enough depth to show facial texture.
BLACK_AND_WHITE.proTip: Look for strong directional light and deep shadows. Contrast is everything in black and white portraits.
BLACK_AND_WHITE.commonMistake: Desaturating color images without remapping tones loses all depth and interest.

REFLECTIONS_PORTRAIT.rationale: Shallow DoF from f/2.8 blurs one plane (reflection or subject) for creative double-image effects.
REFLECTIONS_PORTRAIT.proTip: Focus on the reflection for an ethereal look, or on the real subject and let the reflection blur artistically.
REFLECTIONS_PORTRAIT.commonMistake: Not cleaning the surface—smudges and dust ruin reflections at close proximity.

NEWBORN_STUDIO.rationale: f/5.6 provides enough depth of field for a full newborn body photographed from above. 1/160s ensures no motion blur from startle reflexes. ISO 400 minimises noise without grain visible in soft skin.
NEWBORN_STUDIO.proTip: Use a large octobox or north-facing window at 45 degrees to eliminate harsh shadows. Silent electronic shutter prevents startle reactions. Set white balance warm (3200-4000K) for correct skin tone.
NEWBORN_STUDIO.commonMistake: Using flash at full power. Newborns are photosensitive. Use bounce flash at -2EV or continuous LED lighting at 3200K. Silent shutter is critical — mirror slap causes startle reflex.

EDITORIAL_HIGH_FASHION.rationale: f/8 provides full sharpness from face to fingertips — editorial demands technical perfection throughout. ISO 100 for maximum dynamic range and tonal gradation. 1/160 syncs with studio strobe.
EDITORIAL_HIGH_FASHION.proTip: Use a 1.2-1.4m Profoto octabox at camera-left as key, silver reflector camera-right as fill. Check histogram on every frame. Magazine editors expect technically perfect exposures with no clipping.
EDITORIAL_HIGH_FASHION.commonMistake: Shooting wide open (f/1.4-f/2.8) for fashion — atmospheric, but editorial clients demand sharpness throughout the frame. f/8 is the industry standard for technical fashion photography.

MATERNITY_OUTDOOR.rationale: f/2.8 provides shallow depth of field that separates the subject from background. 1/400s ensures no motion blur in flowing garments. Golden hour side lighting reveals form at its most flattering.
MATERNITY_OUTDOOR.proTip: Shoot 45 minutes before sunset for soft directional light that reveals the silhouette. Position subject so the sun backlights the profile. The bump reads best from the side in warm light.
MATERNITY_OUTDOOR.commonMistake: Shooting at noon with flat overhead light — this removes all form definition. Golden hour or open shade only. Avoid backlit locations where harsh sun hits the camera lens directly.
